coredata.dc:title: Leveraging oxygen-vacancy-rich Yb-MnO2/N-carbon quantum dots heterostructure for solar light-driven tetracycline detoxification and antibiotic-resistant bacteria inactivation

coredata.dc:description: Microbial pollution and antibiotic contamination can pose a serious threat to human health. The rational development of efficient and low-cost methods is critical to solving these problems. In this study, novel Z-scheme heterojunctions consisting of Yb-MnO2 and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were fabricated to achieve efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC) and the destruction of bacteria (Escherichia coli used as a model). Results showed that the Yb-doping strategy improves the characteristics of host MnO2 by weakening the lattice interface and increasing its surface area. Compared with Yb-MnO2, Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterostructures exhibited superior photocatalytic activity in TC degradation under simulated solar light. The remarkable photocatalytic and mild oxidation abilities of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs led to an impressive 97.88% removal rate of TC. Additionally, the structure displayed robust resistance to various ions and broad pH levels. Notably, the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterostructures exhibited excellent bactericidal activity, with no observed bacterial resistance even after 20 consecutive trials. Furthermore, quenching experiments demonstrated that 1O2 and O2 − were the primary radicals involved in the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs-based photocatalysis process. The remarkable photocatalytic activity of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs can be attributed to two key factors. Firstly, the large surface area and abundant oxygen defects provide rich active sites for reactant degradation. Secondly, the Z-scheme heterojunctions formed between Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs effectively facilitate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and increase the reduction/oxidation potentials for reactive oxygen species generation. This study presents a promising novel photocatalyst for the facile and energy-saving treatment of antibiotic and microbial pollution, and it may provide new insight into the design of highly efficient heterogeneous photocatalysts.

The Raman spectra (a), XPS spectra (b), TGA curves (c), and XRD patterns (d) of different samples.
Surface properties of the synthesized MnO2, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs materials. High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Mn 2p, (b) Mn 3s, and (c) O 1s for MnO2, Yb-MnO2 and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis DRS spectra and (b) Tauc plots of (αhν)2 of MnO2, N-CQD, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs composites, respectively. (c) Down-converted PL spectra of N-CQDs; (d) Band structure diagrams of Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs; (e) Band diagram mechanisms for equilibrium in the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs photocatalyst interface. The inset in Fig. 4c shows the photograph of the N-CQD solution under white light (left) and 285 nm UV irradiation (right).

The photoelectrochemical properties of the synthesized MnO2, N-CQDs, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials. (a) Photoluminescence spectra (λex = 320 nm), (b) Transient photocurrent responses, and (c) EIS Nyquist plots of MnO2, N-CQDs, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials.

TC removal performance of synthesized MnO2, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials. (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC solution after reaction; (b) TC removal efficiency with different catalysts over time; (c) TC removal rate of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite with or without oxalic acid, under dark or light irradiation; Influence of pH (d) and ions (100 μM) (e) on the TC removal efficiency; (f) Cycling test for TC removal using Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials.

Cytotoxicity of TC treated with or without Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials. Viability of E. coli (a) and FHC cells (b) incubated with different concentrations of TC and TC treated with Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials.

The disinfection performance of Yb-MnO2 and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials. Morphology change and resistance development in E. coli upon treatment with MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposites. (a, b) Fluorescence images of multi-drug resistant E. coli stained with SYTO 9/propidium iodide (PI) after the photocatalytic disinfection process; (c) SEM images of multi-drug resistant E. coli without (top) and with treatment (bottom); (d) The resistance development assay of E. coli treated with Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs at sublethal concentrations for twenty passages.

Types of radicals generated in the photocatalytic process. (a) The TC removal performance of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs with different scavengers; (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2, Yb-MnO2, Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs, and H2O2 solution after 40 min of illumination; (c) EPR signals of TEMP-1O2 in aqueous dispersion; (d) EPR signals of DMPO-O2 − in methanol dispersion.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs.

head.ce:title: Leveraging oxygen-vacancy-rich Yb-MnO2/N-carbon quantum dots heterostructure for solar light-driven tetracycline detoxification and antibiotic-resistant bacteria inactivation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microbial pollution and antibiotic contamination can pose a serious threat to human health. The rational development of efficient and low-cost methods is critical to solving these problems. In this study, novel Z-scheme heterojunctions consisting of Yb-MnO2 and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were fabricated to achieve efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC) and the destruction of bacteria (Escherichia coli used as a model). Results showed that the Yb-doping strategy improves the characteristics of host MnO2 by weakening the lattice interface and increasing its surface area. Compared with Yb-MnO2, Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterostructures exhibited superior photocatalytic activity in TC degradation under simulated solar light. The remarkable photocatalytic and mild oxidation abilities of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs led to an impressive 97.88% removal rate of TC. Additionally, the structure displayed robust resistance to various ions and broad pH levels. Notably, the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterostructures exhibited excellent bactericidal activity, with no observed bacterial resistance even after 20 consecutive trials. Furthermore, quenching experiments demonstrated that 1O2 and O2 − were the primary radicals involved in the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs-based photocatalysis process. The remarkable photocatalytic activity of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs can be attributed to two key factors. Firstly, the large surface area and abundant oxygen defects provide rich active sites for reactant degradation. Secondly, the Z-scheme heterojunctions formed between Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs effectively facilitate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and increase the reduction/oxidation potentials for reactive oxygen species generation. This study presents a promising novel photocatalyst for the facile and energy-saving treatment of antibiotic and microbial pollution, and it may provide new insight into the design of highly efficient heterogeneous photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last few decades, antibiotics have been extensively used to control bacterial infections in both humans and animals, as well as to promote agricultural growth [1]. Because of the broad-spectrum of activity and low price, tetracycline (TC) is widely used throughout the world [2–4]. However, due to overuse and incomplete metabolism in animals and humans, TC is inevitably released into the environment [5]. It has been widely detected in the aqueous environment, including groundwater, surface water, and sewage water, with concentrations ranging from ng/L to mg/L [6]. Researches show that TC in the environment can cause various potential adverse effects on the ecosystems. Moreover, exposure to TC can lead to the gradual development and spread of antibiotic resistance in pathogenic microorganisms, posing higher risks to human health [7,8]. Therefore, effectively eliminating TC and bacteria of concern is crucial to ensure environmental and ecological safety.

It is well known that TC undergoes photolysis under solar irradiation [9]. The molecular oxygen in the environment can be activated by the light and react with the TC, leading to its mineralization [10]. Although the process is simple and cost-free, its application in TC decontamination could be hindered by the slow reaction rate associated with the spin-forbidden reactions [11]. To improve the reaction kinetics and TC removal efficiency, TiO2 nanomaterials have been employed as photocatalysts for the efficient oxidative decomposition of TC [12]. However, the activation of TiO2 requires ultraviolet or near-ultraviolet irradiation, which encompasses only a mere 4% of the solar spectrum that reaches the Earth's surface [13,14]. Numerous studies have introduced innovative photocatalysts, such as graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), CeO2, Bi2WO6, and MnO2, capable of harnessing a significant fraction of the solar spectrum [15]. Among these alternatives, MnO2 emerges prominently due to its appealing properties including a narrow bandgap, photo-stability, and low-cost [16]. Through the application of diverse synthesis approaches, it becomes feasible to tailor the morphology, crystal structure, and surface oxygen vacancies of MnO2, ultimately leading to an enhancement in the material's catalytic activity. For example, the work of Wenhao Yang et al. revealed that elevating the amount of surface oxygen vacancies in δ-MnO2 could be achieved through the reduction of Mn4+, leading to enhanced visible-light-driven catalysis [17]. However, the relatively rapid recombination rate of photogenerated charge within MnO2 can significantly influence the quantity of generated reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as superoxide anion radical (O2 −), hydroxyl radical (OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2) from O2. This, in turn, exerts further influence on the mineralization efficiency of TC [18].

Incorporating different types of materials into MnO2 to create heterostructures has been demonstrated to be an effective strategy to prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus improving the photo-catalytic activity of the nanomaterial. Materials including g-C3N4, graphene, ZnO, and CoTiO3 have been successfully incorporated into MnO2 to fabricate nanocomposite, which exhibited good performance in antibiotic decontamination under strong visible light irradiation [19–21]. However, their photocatalytic efficiency under solar light irradiation is far from satisfactory for their practical use. In addition, the simultaneous elimination of coexisting high-risk pollutants like pathogens by these photocatalysts has rarely been achieved.

As a carbon-based material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are widely used as photosensitizers and electron mediators to broaden the photon absorption region of the host material [22,23]. Moreover, incorporating heteroatomic N doping into CQDs (N-CQDs) can significantly enhance material conductivity and optical properties, while simultaneously extending the lifetime of charge carriers [24]. For instance, Qi et al. prepared N-CQD-modified p-n heterojunction photocatalysts, N-CQD/BiO2-x/BiOCl, using a facile ion-etching strategy. Differential charge density calculations, photocatalytic performance assessments, and density flooding theory calculations demonstrated that the introduction of N-CQDs into BiO2-x/BiOCl could effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, leading to significant catalytic activity in the photodegradation of sulfonamide antibiotics [25]. Notably, Meziani et al. demonstrated that CQDs exhibit efficient bactericidal effects under visible light illumination [26]. Thus, the incorporation of N-CQDs into MnO2 could potentially not only form Schottky junctions on the surface of the heterostructures to enhance carrier mobility but also improve the bactericidal activity of the material. Recently, several studies have reported the fabrication of N-CQDs/MnO2 nanocomposites as a fluorescence resonance energy transfer platform for detecting a variety of biomolecules [27–29]. Despite the progress, further application of the nanocomposites for the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics and the coexisting high-risk antibiotic-resistant bacteria is hampered by the poor stability of the hybrid structures formed through the electrostatic interaction.

In this study, we aim to fabricate MnO2/N-CQDs heterostructures as highly effective photocatalysts capable of simultaneously eradicating TC and bacteria when exposed to solar light. As catalytic reactions primarily take place on the heterogeneous solid surfaces of photocatalysts, the morphology of MnO2 used to incorporate N-CQDs is expected to significantly influence the performance of the nanocomposite. Ion-doping (e.g., K+, Ce3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Tb3+) has been demonstrated to be an effective and facile strategy to tune the properties of MnO2 nanomaterials [30,31]. The ions can enter the tunnels or framework of the MnO2 structure, leading to alterations in its crystal structure, morphology, electron spin properties, and even surface defects [32]. Based on these findings, we initially attempted to incorporate lanthanide ions (La, Ce, Nd, Sm, Tb, Er, and Yb) into the MnO2 host. Due to the abundant f-orbitals, lanthanide ions possess the capability not just to alter the physicochemical properties of the material, but also to accumulate organic pollutants at the material's surface, thereby enhancing catalytic reactions [33,34]. The strategy was successfully demonstrated, as the TC removal efficiency significantly increased with the doping of lanthanide ions, with Yb3+ being the most effective among the used ions. Subsequently, the coupling of Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs resulted in the formation of heterojunctions, leading to enhanced electron transfer and charge separation efficiency, along with an increased number of surface oxygen vacancies. With their superior properties, Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposites exhibited better photocatalytic performance than Yb-MnO2 nanomaterials. This contributed to the efficient degradation of TC and the inactivation of antibiotic-resistant bacteria (E. coli) under simulated solar light irradiation. Moreover, the radicals produced during the photocatalytic process and the toxicity of TC after solar-light-assisted catalytic treatment were elucidated. Finally, the possible photocatalytic charge transfer mechanism over Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterogeneous structure under sunlight irradiation was proposed based on a series of experiments. This study presents a promising, innovative photocatalyst for facile and energy-saving treatment of antibiotic pollution, and may offer new insights into the design of highly efficient heterogeneous photocatalysts.

KMnO4 was bought from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). MnCl2·4H2O, LnCl3 (Ln = La, Ce, Nd, Sm, Tb, Er, and Yb), KI, potassium biphthalate (C8H5KO4), oxalic acid (C2H2O4), isopropanol (IPA), and L-histidine (L-His) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology (Shanghai, China). Chitosan (C6H11NO4) n, Sodium chromate tetrahydrate (Na2CrO4·4H2O) was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Tetracycline (C22H24N2O8) was purchased from MedChem Express (Shanghai, China). SYTO9/PI Double Stain Kit was obtained from Shanghai BestBio Biology (Shanghai, China). Superoxide dismutase (SOD), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) were obtained from Sigma (Shanghai, China). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were used directly without further purification.

The synthesis details for Ln-MnO2 (Ln = La, Ce, Nd, Sm, Tb, Er, and Yb) and the N-CQDs materials can be found in the supplementary information. Yb-MnO2 was chosen for subsequent studies based on its superior TC removal performance (Fig. S1).

Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterogeneous structures were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal process. KMnO4 (0.192 g) was first dissolved in 20 mL of N-CQDs solution at 80 ℃ under continuous stirring. Then, a mixture comprising YbCl3 (0.037 g) and MnCl2·4H2O (0.327 g) in 4 mL of N-CQDs solution was titrated into the KMnO4/N-CQDs solution. After a 2-hour reaction at 80 ℃, a black-brown precipitate formed, which was separated from the solution by centrifugation (4000 rpm). The resulting precipitate was washed twice with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol before being dried overnight in a vacuum freeze dryer.
Additionally, details on optimizing the performance of the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterogeneous structure by varying the amounts of reactants were shown in the supplementary information.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Smart Lab 9 kW) analysis was used to characterize the crystal structure of materials. Surface properties of the samples were investigated via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), utilizing a Thermo Scientific Al Kα x-ray source. Microscopic morphology of materials was observed through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were obtained using a BET surface and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics Tristar, USA). UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded using a UH-4150 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Thermal stability analysis was conducted with an 1100LF simultaneous thermal analyzer (METTLER, Switzerland) under O2 flow at a ramp rate of 10 K/min within a temperature range of 30 to 600 ℃. Raman spectra were recorded using a Japan Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution with an excitation wavelength of 514 nm. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) signals were recorded using a JES X310 (Nippon Electron). The photoelectrochemical properties of samples were analyzed with an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, China).

The photocatalytic activity of the samples for degrading TC (25 mg/L) under simulated solar light irradiation was assessed. The light source was supplied by a PL-SXE300 xenon lamp, set to an optical density parameter of 0.1 W/cm2 (equivalent to 1 sun intensity) [35], as verified with a PMA2140 global radiometer. To initiate the reaction, 25 mg of the catalyst was introduced into a 50 mL TC aqueous solution. At specific time intervals, 200 μL of the solution was taken out and passed through a 0.22 μm membrane filter. Subsequently, it was subjected to analysis using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-2910) to quantify the absorption peak intensity at 356 nm. In the reusability test, the used photocatalyst was thoroughly rinsed before commencing subsequent TC degradation cycles. The reaction rate constant in the first-order kinetic process is shown in equation (1), while the TC removal rate is determined by equation (2) [36,37].

C0 and Ct (mg/L) represent the concentrations of TC before and after the reaction for t minutes, respectively. k is the pseudo-first-order rate constant, and t is the reaction time in minutes, respectively.
To differentiate between the adsorption and degradation effect, oxalic acid (0.1 mL, 25 mg/mL) was introduced to dissolve the catalyst to release the adsorbed TC.

A Gram-negative strain of Escherichia coli (E. coli) was used as a model bacterium. The bacterial stock solution was first diluted to 1 × 107 CFU/mL and mixed with the autoclaved medium in equal proportions. MnO2, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs at the indicated amount were added, and the samples were exposed to the light at 0.1 W/cm2 for 50 min. Subsequently, 200 μL of the suspension was placed into a 96-well plate. The growth of bacteria was monitored by measuring the cell density (600 nm) every 10 min using a microplate reader (BioTek, USA). Additionally, 100 μL of the serially diluted bacterial solution was spread onto agar plates and then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Bacterial density was analyzed by plate counting method. Control experiments were conducted without photocatalysts and light.

The photoelectrochemical properties of the synthesized materials were assessed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, China), with Pt wire, Ag/AgCl, and ITO serving as the counter electrode, reference electrode, and working electrode, respectively. The photocurrent spectrum (IT) was measured at 0.5 V with a 20-second interval, and the electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was recorded within the frequency range of 0.1–100 KHz, applying a 5 mV amplitude.

The toxicity of TC toward bacteria and cells after photocatalytic treatment by Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials was assessed using E. coli and fetal human colon cells (FHC) as a model, respectively. The FHC cells were inoculated in 96-well plates at a density of 5000/well and incubated with fresh medium containing different concentrations of TC and treated TC solution for 24 h. The cell viability was measured by a CCK-8 kit. E. coli diluted to a concentration of 1 × 107 CFU/mL was subjected to a similar treatment, and their growth was monitored by Synergy HTX Multi-Mode Reader (Biotek, USA).

The incorporation of lanthanide ions (La, Ce, Nd, Sm, Tb, Er, and Yb) into the MnO2 host to enhance the performance of the material was first carried out. As depicted in Fig. S1a, the TC removal efficiency exhibited a substantial improvement following the introduction of lanthanide ions. Notably, among the utilized ions, Yb3+ demonstrated the most effectiveness. The underlying reasons for this phenomenon may be attributed to the electronic structure and ionic radius of the incorporated ions [38], which will be further elucidated in forthcoming research endeavors. Furthermore, the reactant ratio was optimized, and the most effective Yb-MnO2 sample was obtained at a reactant ratio of 0.15 (Yb/Mn) (Fig. S1b). ICP-MS analysis of this material reveals a Yb/Mn atomic ratio of 0.11, suggesting that most Yb3+ has been successfully doped into MnO2 (Fig. S1c). The microstructure and morphology of MnO2 before and after Yb3+ doping were observed by SEM and TEM. Fig. 1 a and d clearly show that pure MnO2 consists of mixed structures, including two-dimensional layers with various polymorphs, as well as one-dimensional nanorods and nanowires. In contrast, Yb-MnO2 nanomaterials exhibit a flower-like spherical structure with a diameter ranging from 300 to 500 nm (Fig. 1b and e). The observed alteration in morphology can potentially be attributed to the influence of Yb3+ ions on the growth of MnO2 through an in-situ metal doping process. Notably, the larger radius of Yb3+ ions (0.985 Å) compared to that of Mn3+ and Mn4+ ions (∼0.75 Å and 0.67 Å, respectively) restricts their incorporation within the MnO6 octahedral framework. As MnO2 grows, primary nanoparticles typically undergo preferentially attachment of MnO6 octahedra and oriented growth until they reach a critical size [39]. Since MnO6 octahedra are abundant in negative charges due to the presence of vacancy sites and Mn3+ [39], they can adsorb Yb3+ ions to balance the negative charges. Once Yb3+ is adsorbed, primary nanoparticles will attract others through Coulombic interactions, thereby facilitating the formation of larger assembled nanospheres [40]. BET analysis was carried out to calculate the specific surface area using nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm (Fig. S2 and Table S1). Both MnO2 and Yb-MnO2 display a type IV isotherm with an IUPAC H3-type hysteresis loop, indicating the mesoporous nature of the materials [41]. The specific surface area of Yb-MnO2 was 266.5 m2/g, which was about 3.9 times that of MnO2 (68.3 m2/g). As photocatalytic processes generally occur on the surface of photocatalysts, the highly porous structure induced by Yb-doping is advantageous for constructing a heterostructured MnO2 catalyst with high catalytic activity.

The fabrication pathway for the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The reaction condition was optimized to obtain the most efficient Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs materials (Figs. S2–S4). The resultant material exhibits type III adsorption isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, similar to those of Yb-MnO2, and features a measured specific surface area of 282.7 m2/g (Fig. S2, Table S1). SEM images suggest that the nanocomposites exhibit a similar size but slightly different morphology compared to that of Yb-MnO2 (Fig. 1c). This suggests that the introduction of N-CQDs did not impact the Yb-MnO2 polymorphs but might have influenced its growth orientation during the hydrothermal process. In Fig. 1f, the lattice fringes at the outer edge of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs appear less ordered and more indeterminate, implying a high dispersion of N-CQDs at the interface. The EDS analysis of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs (Fig. S5) demonstrates an even distribution of Yb, Mn, C, and O elements. HRTEM images of MnO2, Yb-MnO2, and N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1g-i. Specifically, MnO2 and Yb-MnO2 display lattice spacing of 0.26 nm and 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (211) and (001) crystal planes of α-MnO2 and δ-MnO2, respectively. N-CQDs possess a spherical shape with an average size of 3.33 nm (Fig. S6) and exhibit a lattice spacing of 0.28 nm (inset of Fig. 1i). The HRTEM image of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs (Fig. 1j) reveals the presence of the (002) lattice plane of N-CQDs and the (001) crystal plane of MnO2, providing clear evidence of the successful establishment of the heterogeneous structure.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of Mn 2p3/2 for the MnO2, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs samples are shown in Fig. 3 a. The three peaks obtained by deconvolution at 644.0 eV, 642.1 eV, and 640.5 eV correspond to Mn4+, Mn3+, and Mn2+, respectively [46]. Based on the calculated peak areas, it is evident that Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposites have a higher proportion of low-valent Mn (Mn3+ and Mn2+) compared to Yb-MnO2 (Table 1). It is known that oxygen vacancies are generated to balance the charge due to the reduction of Mn4+. The increase in low-valent Mn induces the formation of crystal defects and oxygen vacancies in Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs [47,48]. The average oxidation state (AOS) of surface Mn was calculated using the following equation: AOS = 8.956–1.126ΔE, where ΔE denotes the cleavage width of Mn 3s [17,49]. The AOS corresponding to MnO2, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs, were 3.09, 3.06, and 2.7, respectively (Fig. 3b), further verifying the presence of a significant amount of low-valent Mn in the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs material. Manganese, in its low valent state, tends to form longer and weaker bonds with oxygen (Mn-O) on the surface [50]. These bonds are more susceptible to breaking, thereby facilitating the release of oxygen atoms for the oxidation process. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s can be fitted to three peaks at 529.6, 531.6, and 533.2 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen (Olatt), surface adsorbed oxygen (Oads) and surface adsorbed H2O (OH 2 O), respectively (Fig. 3c) [51]. As shown in Table 1, the proportion of adsorbed oxygen on the surface of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs is significantly higher, at approximately 78.13%, compared to Yb-MnO2 (62.49%) and MnO2 (23.99%). Given that oxygen molecules tend to be adsorbed onto the oxygen vacancies present in oxide materials, this observation is in good agreement with the result from the Mn 2p3/2 XPS investigations. As adsorbed oxygen can be preferentially activated through the transformation by oxygen vacancy [52], Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs likely exhibit high activity in photocatalytic reactions.

The optical properties of N-CQDs, MnO2, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs were characterized using UV–Vis diffuse-reflectance spectroscopy (Fig. 4 a). The band gap of each nanomaterial can be calculated via the Kubelk-Munk method.
(3)

1 / n

A
h υ - E g

Α is the absorption coefficient, h is Planck's constant, υ is the optical frequency, and Eg is the bandgap energy, using an exponent n equal ½ attributed to a direct allowed transition [53]. By plotting (αhυ)1/n versus hυ, the bandgap energies of N-CQDs, MnO2, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials were determined to be 2.92, 2.13, 1.93, and 1.62 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b). To clarify the broad-spectrum response of N-CQDs, their down-converted PL spectra were examined with various excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 4c, N-CQDs exhibit favorable visible emission (400 nm–600 nm) when exposed to light with an excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 500 nm. Excitation-dependent photoluminescence has been observed in many types of CQDs. This phenomenon could be ascribed to the selective excitation of subsets of CQDs within the CQD ensemble, as well as to multiple active and independently excitable emission sites within a single CQD [54]. This property enables the material the form of electron-hole pairs under broad-spectrum sunlight irradiation, therefore enhancing the photocatalytic activity of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs. Fig. S7a and b show the UPS spectra of Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs, where the valence band potentials (EVB) of Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs are 1.53 eV and 4.33 eV, respectively. Following the equation (ECB vs. NHE = EVB − Eg), the ECB of Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs are calculated to be 1.41 eV and −0.4 eV, respectively. The work functions (Φ) of Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs were measured using UPS to explicate the interfacial charge migration dynamics, types, and direction between Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. S7c and d, the secondary electron cutoff energies (E cut-off) of Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs are 16.00 eV and 17.60 eV, respectively. By subtracting the excitation energy of He I (21.22 eV), Φ is calculated to be 5.2 eV and 3.6 eV for Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs, respectively. The energy band schematics of Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 4d, indicating the possible existence of Z-scheme photocatalytic heterostructure between Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs structure.

Due to the difference in Fermi levels (Ef), the energy band could bend. When Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs come into intimate contact, a built-in electric field forms at the interface, resulting in a Schottky barrier and upward bending of the energy band (Fig. 4e) [55]. This virtually prevents the backflow of electrons, allowing the photogenerated electron-hole pairs to separate effectively. Under light excitation, electrons from N-CQDs transition to the conduction band (CB) and travel through the bent energy band to reach the valence band (VB) of Yb-MnO2. There, they combine with holes before transitioning to the CB of Yb-MnO2. Hence, the successful synthesis of a Z-scheme photocatalytic heterostructure comprising Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs enables effective regulation of charge redistribution at the interface.

The effectiveness of photoinduced charge separation and transfer significantly influences the photoelectrochemical characteristics of a material. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy was thus recorded, and it's known that charge separation efficiency is inversely proportional to PL intensity [39]. As shown in Fig. 5 a, both MnO2 and Yb-MnO2 display a high PL intensity, indicating rapid carrier recombination rates. In contrast, Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs show a lower PL intensity, implying that improved charge transport restricts the recombination of electrons and holes. In addition, the interfacial charge transfer behavior of the catalysts was characterized by photocurrent and EIS measurements. In repeated on/off light cycles (Fig. 5b), Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs exhibit the highest photocurrent density among MnO2 and Yb-MnO2, reflecting superior carrier separation and migration in the heterojunction, as well as efficient suppression of charge recombination. In Fig. 5c, the charge transfer efficiency is visually presented through the EIS Nyquist curve, where a smaller arc radius indicates lower charge transfer resistance [56]. Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite exhibits lower resistance than Yb-MnO2, further suggesting that the formation of heterostructures has enhanced the separation and mobility of charge carriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of TC by Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the impressive physicochemical performance of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite, its catalytic efficacy in the degradation of TC under simulated solar light is evaluated. Fig. 6 a presented UV–visible spectra of the TC solution (25 mg/L) subsequent to its treatment with MnO2, Yb-MnO2 and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials, respectively. Notably, the absorption peak at 356 nm is a characteristic peak of TC. Its pronounced reduction follows a discernible sequence: Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs > Yb-MnO2 > MnO2. These observations indicate that the Yb-doping strategy exerts influence not only on the morphology and structure of MnO2 but also on the enhancement of its photocatalytic activity. Specifically, the TC removal efficiency of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs is 2.03 times and 1.12 times greater than that of MnO2 and Yb-MnO2, respectively. The dynamic of the process was further evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6b, the adsorption of TC onto the MnO2, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs materials reached equilibrium following a 10 min stirring in darkness, yielding removal rates of 19.07%, 61.03% and 75.66%, respectively. Notably, these values correlate well with the specific surface areas of these nanomaterials, as detailed in Table S1. Following exposure to light for 20 min, the TC removal efficiency for MnO2, Yb-MnO2, and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs increased to 40.94%, 81.55%, and 98.77%, respectively. To confirm the potential degradation of TC adsorbed on the surface, Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs employed in the TC removal experiment under dark and light conditions were subsequently subjected to treatment with oxalic acid (25 mM). This resulted in the dissolution of samples and the release of 53.18% and 13.1% of the adsorbed TC (Fig. 6c). Interestingly, the TC removal efficiency of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs in the absence of light reached 80.99%. Therefore, even upon excluding the adsorption effect (53.18%), a notable 27.81% of TC undergoes degradation in darkness, which could potentially be attributed to the oxidative properties of MnO2 [57]. Taken together, the high adsorption efficiency of the material could facilitate TC to participate in the catalytic reactions that mainly take place on heterogeneous solid surfaces. Photodegradation notably contributes to the predominant TC removal performance, accounting for a 59.09% degradation during the reaction. Additionally, the material-driven oxidation contributes 27.81%, while adsorption contributes 13.1% to the overall TC removal rate under light irradiation.

The performance of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterogeneous structure may be affected by wastewater parameters, including pH and ions. Therefore, the impact of these crucial parameters on TC elimination efficiency via photocatalytic degradation is investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 6d, the system maintains a TC removal rate of over 97.1% within a pH range from 5.0 to 9.0, which covers the common pH values found in actual wastewater. At pH levels of 3.0 and 11.0, the maximum TC removal rates decreased by approximately 15% and 28%, respectively. To evaluate the influence of ions commonly present in wastewater, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, and PO4 3− ions were introduced into the reaction system. As shown in Fig. 6e, the presence of these ions does not have a substantial impact on the TC removal efficiency. This photocatalytic system exhibits superior resistance to the influence of coexisting ions and water pH compared to other reported photocatalysts, highlighting its potential for practical application in TC pollution treatment. Furthermore, the potential for reutilization of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials is assessed (Fig. 6f). The results reveal that even after four consecutive usages, the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs maintains good stability, with TC removal rates of 98.77%, 94.16%, 94.10%, and 89.69%, respectively. The slight decrease in the TC removal efficiency may be ascribed to either the depletion of the photocatalyst or the blockage of active sites on the material surface.

Antibiotics in the environment can cause bacteria to develop resistance. Therefore, the toxicity of TC towards bacteria after photocatalytic treatment with Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials was evaluated using E. coli as a model. The results demonstrate significant growth inhibition of bacteria at a TC concentration of 0.2 μg/mL and near-complete bacterial eradication at 1 μg/mL (Fig. 7 a). Nevertheless, the growth of bacteria remained unaffected when exposed to treated TC solution even at a high concentration (1 μg/mL). Ingestion is considered a significant route of human exposure to contaminants. Fetal human colon cells (FHC) were thus utilized to evaluate the health risks of TC pollutants. As shown in Fig. 7b, TC displayed remarkable cytotoxic effects on FHC cells, with the number of viable cells decreasing to 4.3% at a concentration of 25 μg/mL. Notably, the toxicity of TC significantly decreased following the treatment, with 72.8% of cells remaining alive at the same concentration (25 μg/mL). These results demonstrate the efficient elimination of TC toxicity through the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs catalyzed degradation process under simulated solar light irradiation.

The effectiveness of the prepared heterostructures in disinfecting under solar light irradiation was evaluated by measuring their ability to inactivate E. coli. As shown in Fig. S8, MnO2 had only a minor impact on bacterial viability. At a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, the density of E. coli subjected to material treatment, both with and without light irradiation, remained at 74.64% and 84.84% of the control group's density, respectively. Based on the bacterial growth curves shown in Fig. 8 a and b, it can be inferred that treating E. coli cells with Yb-MnO2 and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs in darkness resulted in a reduction in viability by 36.1% and 72.1%, respectively. This indicates that the oxidative capacity of the materials observed in the TC removal experiment also influences bacterial viability. The oxidative activity of the synthesized materials follows this order: Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs > Yb-MnO2 > MnO2, which aligns with the surface Mn3+ content in each material. Previous research has demonstrated a positive correlation between surface Mn3+ concentration and the oxidative reactivity of MnO2 materials, attributed to the elongated Mn–O bond and the presence of antibonding electrons in surface Mn3+ [57]. The more pronounced effect of MnO2/N-CQDs on bacterial inactivation than on TC degradation in darkness could be attributed to the effect of N-CQD. N-CQDs synthesized in this study carry a positive charge at pH 7.0, with a measured zeta potential of 4.43 mV (Fig. S9). Positively charged CQDs are known for their inherent bactericidal properties, as they can bind to bacteria, leading to membrane destabilization and substantial mechanical damage to the cell membrane [58]. Moreover, upon exposure to light irradiation, the bactericidal efficiency of Yb-MnO2 and Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs further increased from 36.1% and 72.1% to 40.0% and 86.6%, respectively. This observation suggests that the enhanced photocatalytic activity by the heterogeneous structure further intensifies the antibacterial efficacy of the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials.

The significant antibacterial activity of the heterostructures is further validated by their ability to combat drug-resistant E. coli. About 61.1% and 87.2% of the drug-resistant strains are inactivated under dark and light-exposed conditions, respectively (Fig. 8c, d). The bacteria death was further evaluated by live/dead bacterial staining assays. Without Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs, almost all bacteria were stained only with green fluorescence, indicating that the cells remained alive (Fig. 9 a). Nevertheless, upon treatment with the photocatalyst, the cells exhibited bright red fluorescence, indicating the eradication of most bacterial cells (Fig. 9b). Changes in cell morphology and membrane integrity of multidrug-resistant E. coli before and after treatment with Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs were observed using SEM. As shown in Fig. 9c, multidrug-resistant E. coli retained smooth rod-like morphology without Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite, and no cell membrane damage was observed. In contrast, after disinfection treatment by the material, most of the bacteria collapsed with obvious morphology changes. Notably, the development of resistance in E. coli toward this treatment was investigated. In the assay, E. coli was subjected to 20 consecutive cycles of treatment with a sublethal concentration of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs, and the MIC values were assessed after each cycle. As shown in Fig. 9d, the MICn/MIC1 values showed minimal change compared to the control over 20 passages, suggesting that E. coli did not develop resistance. These results highlight the superiority of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs materials in antibacterial applications under solar light irradiation.

To clarify the potential mechanisms responsible for the enhancement of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs materials in TC degradation and bacterial inactivation upon light irradiation, active substances generated in the reaction were analyzed. To eliminate reactive species like 1O2, OH− and e−, L-His (25 mM), IPA (25 mM), and Cr (VI) (10 mM) were employed, whereas SOD (400 U/mL) was utilized as O2 − radical scavengers [59,60]. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the scavengers were observed to inhibit TC degradation at varying levels, indicating the production of reactive species including 1O2, O2 −, OH, and e− during the photocatalytic degradation of TC. As demonstrated in Fig. S10, the rate constant of the reaction upon the addition of L-His (0.0247 min−1), SOD (0.0330 min−1), IPA (0.0580 min−1), and Cr (VI) (0.0465 min−1) were all decreased compared to the control experiment, where TC was removed at a rate of 0.1193 min−1. With the addition of L-His, SOD, IPA, and Cr (VI), the TC removal efficiency decreased significantly by 35.1%, 24.6%, 7.7%, and 13.3%, respectively. This suggests that the primary active species in the reactions were 1O2 and O2 −. Iodometry was used to investigate the generation of H2O2. By detecting I3 − characteristic absorbance peak (350 nm), the presence of H2O2 with a concentration of 340 μM was identified after 40 min of light irradiation (Fig. 10b). EPR spectroscopy was further carried out to confirm the presence of main radicals participating in the photocatalytic reactions. DMPO and TEMP were used to trap O2 − and 1O2, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10c and d, the signal of O2 − and 1O2 was scarcely observed in the dark. In contrast, strong signals of O2 − and 1O2 appeared after light irradiation, with signal intensity increasing as irradiation time increased, further confirming the generation of these reactive species.

Based on the optical and photoelectrochemical properties of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite, along with radicals quenching experiments and EPR test, we propose that the prepared Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite forms a full-spectrum-driven Z-scheme photocatalytic system (Scheme 2 ). Under light irradiation, Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs are excited, generating electron-hole pairs. At the same time, electron transfer occurs. Two mechanisms for charge transfer are generally recognized: the traditional model and the Z scheme mechanism [7,61,62]. In the traditional heterojunction model, the photogenerated electrons transfer from the CB of Yb-MnO2 to the CB of N-CQDs, while the photogenerated holes on the VB of N-CQDs transfer to the VB of Yb-MnO2, facilitating effective separating of photogenerated electrons and holes (Fig. S11). However, photogenerated electrons in N-CQDs cannot reduce O2 to O2 − because the standard redox potentials of O2/O2 − (−0.33 eV) are more negative than that of N-CQDs (1.41 eV). Meanwhile, H2O cannot be oxidized into OH, as the VB potential of H2O/OH (2.38 eV) is more positive than that of Yb-MnO2 (1.53 eV). Given the detection of radicals in the photocatalytic reaction and the important role of O2 − and 1O2 in TC degradation, this mechanism could not be used to explain the high photocatalytic activity of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite. Therefore, a Z-Scheme heterojunction mechanism is proposed. Due to the difference in Fermi levels of Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs (Fig. 4e), an interfacial electron transfer process occurs. Thus, electrons in the CB of N-CQDs can migrate and recombine with holes in the VB of Yb-MnO2 through the heterojunction, resulting in retained holes and electrons with increased reduction/oxidation potentials. On the surface of the heterogeneous material, O2 reacts with the photogenerated electrons in the CB of Yb-MnO2 to form O2 −, which can also transform into 1O2 by reacting with the h+ on the VB of N-CQDs. Meanwhile, part of h+ can oxidize H2O/OH− to OH. Eventually, these generated radicals participate in TC degradation and bacteria inactivation.

Based on the analysis and the physiochemical characterizations presented above, the high photocatalytic activity of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs can be attributed to two factors. Firstly, the significant surface area and abundant oxygen defects provide numerous active sites for the efficient degradation of reactants. Secondly, the Z-scheme heterojunctions between Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs effectively promote the photogenerated electron-hole pairs separation, leading to increased reduction/oxidation potentials for generating reactive oxygen species. A performance comparison between Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs and materials utilized in earlier studies has been outlined in Table S2. It elucidates that the photocatalytic activity of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs surpasses that of the reported catalyst derived from MnO2 and is either comparable or superior to the catalyst derived from TiO2 in terms of TC elimination and bacterial inactivation. The noteworthy photocatalytic activity, recycling stability, anti-interference capability, and energy-efficient decontamination process make Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs well-prepared for practical applications. To enhance the technology's durability and user-friendliness, additional advancements are required in the coating/immobilization process for Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanomaterials.

In summary, unique Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterostructures with outstanding photocatalytic and bactericidal activity were successfully fabricated. The doping of Yb atoms in MnO2 can adjust its structure, resulting in the creation of a porous material with a larger surface area, providing a greater number of active sites. Compared with Yb-MnO2, Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite maintains its porous structure and displays a higher quantity of low-valent Mn and surface oxygen vacancies. The Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterostructures exhibited excellent performance (97.88% removal rate) for TC detoxication under simulated sunlight irradiation. Fascinatingly, it also demonstrated resistance to various ions and broad pH levels. The Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs heterostructures also exhibited remarkable bactericidal activity, and the treatment was demonstrated not to cause resistance in bacteria. Furthermore, quenching experiments demonstrated that 1O2 and O2 − were the main radicals participating in the Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs-based photocatalysis process. The high photocatalytic activity of Yb-MnO2/N-CQDs was proposed to be attributed not only to the large surface area and abundant oxygen defects but also to the Z-scheme heterojunctions formed between Yb-MnO2 and N-CQDs, which effectively promote the photogenerated electron-hole pairs separation. This study provides a low-cost and efficient potential candidate for reducing the risk of antibiotic-containing wastewater in an energy-saving way.
